A buddy I trust told me, "Iran should know it can’t get control over Hormuz with 1-2 missiles attack."

I'm 100% aligned on that. Tehran seems to be trying to flex and claw back control over the strait since the last long weekend, but I seriously question their stomach for real risk here.

For starters, the US pulling the sanctions waiver is pretty much a nothingburger. That 60 day exemption clock was already ticking down to August 21st anyway.

Most refiners had already locked in their August arrival slates, and a 60 day window is way too tight for legal teams to clear internal compliance hurdles and shipping logistics.

Since the bulk of that Iranian flow was front-running straight to China regardless of the waiver, today's headline does zip to the physical balances. Unless Washington rolls out a hard naval blockade, nothing really changes.

But look, everyone on the street knows Trump's default setting is the TACO trade, and I don't see him pulling off any hardline measures. Even if he tries to flex, he'll just TACO right back out.

That leaves Iran as the only player left to force a tail risk scenario, but do they actually want to jump off that cliff? 

The data doesn't lie—as the charts show, they basically sat on their hands and let the barrels clear even while the oil was steadily flowing.

Everyone can probably agree we were well overdue for a tactical bounce anyway, but the reality is that it feels nowhere near enough to fundamentally shift the broader picture.

Are they really going to push past a simple show of strength for negotiating leverage? Hmmm....

Just in: Overseas Research and Selection Daily 0706: Morgan Stanley: The US tariff rate may be stable at 9%-10%;.

Morgan Stanley: The U.S. tariff framework shifts to Sections 301 and 232, and the tax rate may stabilize at 9%-10% Morgan Stanley’s latest report points out that U.S. tariff policy is shifting from a temporary tool to a more legally stable framework of Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 and Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962.

With the Section 122 tariffs set to expire on July 24 and the Section 301 hearing scheduled for July 7, the U.S. tariff system will enter a critical window for reconstruction.

The bank predicts that the statutory effective tariff rate will stabilize at 9%-10% in the next few months, which is more regular than the previous high-dispersion system based on IEEPA, but industry coverage, exemptions, bilateral agreements, refunds and import structures will still affect the actual tax burden.

Morgan Stanley believes that Section 301 will become a broad, country-level basic tariff tool. The 301 investigation on forced labor issues plans to impose additional tariffs on imported goods from 60 economies: the tax rate for economies with relevant import bans or reciprocity commitments is 10%, and the rest are 12.5%.

However, USMCA compliant products, products covered by Section 232, and some textiles, information materials, etc. are exempted. Section 301 helps maintain the tariff floor and bargaining chips, but it is difficult to promote the reshoring of large-scale manufacturing on its own.

Article 232 will assume the function of targeted industrial policy, focusing on strategic areas such as steel, aluminum, copper and its derivatives, automobiles, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, medical equipment, machinery, robots, and aerospace.

Tariffs on metal derivatives will be levied on the full customs declaration value of the product rather than just the metal content. Morgan Stanley estimates that expanding coverage will subject approximately $150 billion of goods to a 25% tariff and increase the overall tax rate by up to about 1 percentage point.

The actual burden of pharmaceutical tariffs depends on factors such as generic drugs, most-favored nation pricing, local investment commitments, and origin. In terms of refunds, the customs has opened the application channel. Review and payment usually take 60-90 days, but refunds are not completed automatically.

Morgan Stanley estimates that the upper limit of refunds will be about US$133 billion. Under the neutral scenario, about 60% of eligible tariffs, or US$84 billion, can be refunded, and in the high scenario, about US$112 billion.

The decline in tariff uncertainty will marginally support capital expenditures, but the bank emphasized that current investment is still concentrated in AI, semiconductors, data centers and power infrastructure, and evidence of the return of traditional manufacturing industries is still limited; the subsequent 232 investigation on machinery and robots will be an important node to test whether tariffs can truly expand manufacturing investment.

Goldman Sachs is bearish on the yen: it may fall to 165 in 12 months, AI investment and energy disturbances support the dollar Goldman Sachs further downgraded the outlook for the yen in its latest research report.

It is expected that the yen will remain under pressure against the US dollar under the combined influence of factors such as the maintenance of high U.S. interest rates, the moderate pace of interest rate hikes by the Bank of Japan, and Japan's fiscal expansion.

The bank raised its forecasts for the dollar against the yen in the next three months, six months and 12 months to 162, 163 and 165 respectively, significantly higher than the previous 160, 158 and 155.

Goldman Sachs believes that if the Japanese government intervenes in the currency market again, it may only be able to temporarily alleviate the depreciation of the yen. Historical experience shows that intervention can usually only temporarily interrupt the rise of the U.S. dollar against the yen. If the U.S.

economy does not experience an unexpected downturn, or the Bank of Japan does not shift to a more aggressive tightening policy, it will be difficult for the yen to form a sustained rebound. The bank pointed out that U.S.

yields are "higher and longer," recession risks are low, fiscal concerns remain, and the Bank of Japan's slow normalization of policy creates a macro backdrop for the yen's continued weakness. At the same time, Japan's fiscal stimulus plan may push up the term premium of its national debt, and changes in relative U.S.

debt have often coincided with further gains in the dollar against the yen in the past. Goldman Sachs still maintains a strong judgment on the U.S. dollar overall. The core basis is that the two major supporting factors of the U.S. AI investment boom and energy supply disruption are not expected to fade quickly.

The bank believes that the continued expansion of AI capital expenditures and recurring energy supply risks will continue to increase the attractiveness of U.S. dollar assets and put low-yielding currencies under interest rate differential pressure for a longer period of time.

Against this background, Goldman Sachs lowered its forecast for the euro, predicting that the euro against the dollar will be 1.14 in three months, fall back to 1.12 in six months, and maintain this level in 12 months.

In contrast, the bank is optimistic about some high-yielding emerging market currencies and has raised its forecast for the Indian rupee, citing improved growth, falling inflation and policy-driven capital inflows.

It is also more optimistic about the Colombian peso, due to the local central bank's hawkish stance and increased fiscal consolidation expectations. Therefore, Goldman Sachs continues to recommend using the Japanese yen as a financing currency to allocate high-yield emerging market assets, betting that low-yield currency financing transactions will continue.

UBS: The digital euro will be launched as early as 2029, and the adoption rate in Northern and Southern Europe may be significantly different.

The latest report from UBS pointed out that after the European Parliament formed a common position on the digital euro, the relevant legislation has entered the final tripartite negotiation stage with the European Commission and the Council.

If an agreement is reached in the second half of the year, regulations may be implemented in the second half of 2026, and the European Central Bank can launch a pilot in 2027 and officially launch a digital euro as early as 2029.

By then, the European Central Bank is expected to become the first major Western central bank to issue retail digital currency for residents and businesses. The digital euro will serve as the legal currency for daily payments, and merchants need to accept it in principle, and its function is similar to cash.

In order to prevent large-scale outflows of bank deposits, the individual holding limit is expected to be set at 3,000 euros without interest. Even if all households hold up to the limit, the size of the digital euro will only account for about 6% of euro area bank deposits, about 800 billion to 1 trillion euros.

The ECB hopes to use this to maintain public access to central bank currency amid a decline in cash use, reduce dependence on foreign payment institutions and stablecoins, and enhance the autonomy of the European payment system. However, UBS believes that the actual adoption rate of the digital euro may be uneven.

In a market where existing electronic payments are highly convenient, consumers lack clear motivation to switch from bank deposits to digital euros; and the Eurozone bank deposit insurance limit of 100,000 euros also weakens the safety advantage of central bank currency.

The report shows that the Eurozone may show a clear "North-South divide": Bank account coverage in Nordic countries is generally close to or exceeds 98%, and cash payment accounts for a low proportion.

The digital euro needs to compete with mature and efficient private payment systems; Southern European countries such as Italy, Greece, and Portugal have relatively low account coverage and stronger cash payment habits, and promotion requires replacing entrenched cash usage scenarios.

UBS believes that the difference in financial account penetration and cash payment preferences will become a key variable in determining the effect of the implementation of the digital euro.

Citi: The Hormuz disturbance subsides, the oil market returns to the logic of oversupply, and Brent oil may fall to $60-65 by the end of the year Citi's latest report pointed out that with the gradual recovery of shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and the high probability of the continuation of the memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran, the geo-risk premium is fading, and the oil market is once again dominated by weak fundamentals.

Brent crude oil has fallen back to the low $70s, and Citigroup expects it to fall to $60-65 a barrel by the end of the year. Citigroup believes that disputes between the United States and Iran over strait management rights and tolls may still recur, but the two sides lack the motivation to escalate the conflict again.

Current AIS data shows that crude oil shipments in the Strait of Hormuz are close to 7 million barrels per day and product oil shipments exceed 1 million barrels per day.

Although they are still lower than the pre-conflict levels of 15 million barrels per day for crude oil and 5 million barrels per day for refined products, some ships have turned off their transponders and the actual flow rate may be higher.

Iran's potential unfreezing of approximately $12 billion in frozen assets also strengthens its willingness to keep shipping lanes open. However, supply is recovering faster than refinery demand is improving.

Chinese buyers have not yet returned significantly, and their crude oil market pull has decreased by more than 6 million barrels per day compared with the February high; global strategic reserves have shifted from absorbing 1.5 million barrels per day to a net supply of more than 2 million barrels to the commercial market.

The spot market has weakened, and the structure of Brent in recent months has turned into a futures premium, with spot Brent oil once falling to US$68.79/barrel; the discount of Angola's Dalia crude oil to spot Brent expanded to nearly US$9/barrel, a record low.

Refined oil cracking spreads are still strong, mainly due to delays in the resumption of refinery operations and disruptions in Russian refining capacity.

However, Citigroup believes that actual refining profits are weaker than the market: forward cash profits in Europe are about US$5/barrel lower than the market, and in Asia are about US$10/barrel, which inhibits refineries from increasing crude oil purchases. The operating rate of U.S.

refineries is close to 95%, and there is limited room for further improvement. Citi predicts that strategic reserve replenishment will be difficult to sustainably absorb excess supply; China's inventory management has become more commercialized, and non-OECD economies are also subject to fiscal and storage capacity constraints. U.S.

liquid fuel production has increased by nearly 1 million barrels per day year-on-year as of April, and crude oil production has increased by approximately 350,000 barrels per day.

The report believes that if OPEC+ does not strengthen supply discipline, supply resilience, saturated refinery demand and weak Chinese demand will continue to put pressure on the oil market until 2027.

Goldman Sachs: AI investment will expand from computing infrastructure to the real economy, and capital demand may exceed US$7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031 Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest report that AI is simultaneously promoting the expansion of digital infrastructure and the reconstruction of the real economy.

Manufacturing, energy, robotics, national defense and industrial software will become the core implementation scenarios in the next stage.

It is estimated that global AI capital expenditures around computing power, data centers and electricity will total approximately US$7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031; of which annual investment will rise from US$765 billion in 2026 to US$1.64 trillion in 2031. Hyperscale cloud vendors may invest more than US$6 trillion in the AI field by 2030.

Goldman Sachs believes that the current impact of AI is first reflected in the software industry, but the opportunities are not limited to software. AI is expected to expand the addressable market for automation and enterprise software by approximately 2.5 times over the next decade.

However, the software industry is also facing a revaluation of valuations and business models: as of June this year, the iShares Expanded Technology Software ETF has fallen by about 17%, down about 26% from its October 2025 high.

The market value of the top ten constituent stocks has decreased by nearly US$800 billion, and the forward price-to-earnings ratio has compressed from about 35 times to 22 times.

The report points out that as basic models gradually become commoditized, pricing power will migrate to "result delivery, agent orchestration, proprietary data and business scenarios." Chinese models are rapidly expanding their share with lower inference costs.

OpenRouter data shows that their token consumption proportion has increased from low single digits at the end of 2024 to about half in early 2026, but the value will ultimately be concentrated in the data layer and result layer.

In the real economy, digital twins, industrial AI, predictive maintenance, logistics automation and robots are accelerating their implementation.

The Siemens Amberg factory has reduced downtime by 40% through AI predictive maintenance; the Figure robot has been running at the BMW factory for a total of about 1,250 hours and participated in the production of more than 30,000 cars.

Goldman Sachs predicts that the global humanoid robot market will grow from 20,000 units in 2025 to 1.4 million units in 2035, but large-scale commercialization is still subject to workflow integration, unit economics and financing structures.

Goldman Sachs emphasized that existing financing tools are still difficult to cover the high capital and long-term deployment needs of physical AI. Private credit, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, ABS, CMBS and new joint venture structures will become key sources of funding.

Industrial AI software mergers and acquisitions and capital integration in robotics, national defense and energy infrastructure are expected to accelerate simultaneously.

Nomura: AI demand supports tight storage supply and demand, and new investment is difficult to change the boom pattern in the short term Nomura said in its latest research report that the market's concerns about the large-scale expansion of Korean memory chip manufacturers and Meta's planned sale of excess computing power may be amplified.

Neither of them is sufficient to indicate that the AI-driven storage boom cycle is weakening. The bank believes that the core contradiction in the current industry is still the tight supply caused by AI demand, rather than short-term overcapacity.

Recently, South Korean memory chip companies and related companies disclosed long-term investment plans with a total scale of approximately 4,800 trillion won, equivalent to approximately US$3.5 trillion, of which approximately 3,700 trillion won was directly invested in storage-related projects.

Relevant news has triggered market concerns about the rapid expansion of future supply of DRAM and NAND. However, Nomura pointed out that it usually takes many years for new projects to be announced, park construction and effective production capacity formed.

Taking the Yongren Semiconductor Industrial Park as an example, the project was launched about nine years ago and is expected to start small-scale production until the end of 2027 at the earliest. This means that the cycle from early investment to the actual release of influential supply may exceed ten years.

Therefore, the recently announced investment plans are unlikely to significantly change the supply and demand pattern of the industry in the next few years. Nomura believes that the storage market is still facing a structural shortage driven by AI demand.

Storage manufacturers are prioritizing the allocation of production capacity for high-bandwidth memory (HBM) with higher profit margins, squeezing the supply space of traditional DRAM and NAND.

As the construction of AI servers, inference applications and data centers continues to advance, the supply constraints of general storage products are likely to continue.

Regarding Meta’s plan to commercialize surplus data center computing power, Nomura believes that this is more like a regular move by large platforms to increase return on capital, rather than a signal of weakening demand for AI hardware.

The bank pointed out that the external sale of computing power can provide support for customers such as OpenAI and Anthropic, and expand the use of model training and inference by reducing computing costs.

Nomura predicts that the demand expansion brought about by the decline in computing power prices may continue to push up the utilization rate of AI infrastructure and maintain demand support for storage products such as HBM, DRAM and NAND.

Goldman Sachs: AI servers drive the boom of MLCC and ABF, raising target prices of Samsung Electro-Mechanics and LG Innotek Goldman Sachs’ latest research report states that the rising demand for high-power, high-speed transmission and high-reliability electronic components in AI servers is pushing MLCC, multi-layer packaging substrate ABF and silicon

capacitors into a new boom cycle; with the popularization of edge AI and physical AI, the electronic component industry may still be in the early stages of the cycle.

Goldman Sachs predicts that the growth in AI server shipments, increased single-machine MLCC usage and product specification upgrades will cause Samsung Electro-Mechanics' (SEMCO) AI server MLCC revenue to increase 6.4 times from 2025 to 2028 to 3.29 trillion won, accounting for 28% of its MLCC revenue.

Driven by the increase in the proportion of high-end products, its MLCC comprehensive ASP is expected to increase from US$3.5 per thousand in 2025 to US$6 in 2028, and its operating profit margin will increase from 12% to 35%.

Despite weak demand for consumer electronics, the company's MLCC capacity utilization rate is expected to remain above 90% in the next three years.

In terms of ABF substrates, Goldman Sachs believes that the AI chip area has expanded and the number of layers has increased, while the industry’s new production capacity is limited, and supply and demand will remain tight for at least 18 to 24 months.

Samsung Electro-Mechanics said that the current demand for ABF is more than 50% higher than its production capacity, and it is expected to reach full production in the second half of 2026.

Goldman Sachs predicts that its ABF revenue will increase from 1.2 trillion won in 2025 to 3.94 trillion won in 2028, and its operating profit margin will rise from 9% to 35%; the AI IC substrate market size may have a compound growth rate of 66% from 2025 to 2028. Silicon capacitors have also become a new attraction.

Samsung Electro-Mechanics has received an order worth 1.6 trillion won from 2027 to 2028. Goldman Sachs predicts that its silicon capacitor revenue will reach approximately 1.5 trillion won in 2028. Goldman Sachs raised Samsung Electro-Mechanics' profit forecasts by 10%, 41% and 96% respectively from 2026 to 2028.

The target price was significantly raised from 1 million won to 2.6 million won, maintaining "buy". For LG Innotek, Goldman Sachs predicts that its ABF revenue will increase from 50 billion won in 2025 to 700 billion won in 2028, but it will still only account for about 2.5% of total revenue, and there is still.

Just in: Overseas Research and Selection Daily 0707: Morgan Stanley: Semiconductor momentum weakens and US stock market gains will spread to other sectors.

Morgan Stanley: Semiconductor momentum weakens, and U.S. stock gains will spread to consumer discretionary, transportation and biotechnology Morgan Stanley's latest research report points out that the change in style of the U.S.

stock market is accelerating: the momentum of the semiconductor sector has weakened after experiencing a historic rise, and ultra-large-scale cloud manufacturers have digested the risks of high capital expenditure growth in advance.

Funds are expected to further flow to the previously relatively lagging optional consumption, transportation, regional banks and biotechnology sectors. The bank believes that the spread of market leadership will continue, but due to adjustments in heavyweight stocks in the market, major stock indexes may remain volatile and weak in the short term.

Morgan Stanley said a significant fall in oil prices would help stabilize interest rate markets and reduce the need for further policy tightening.

The bank expects that falling energy prices, peaking inflationary pressures caused by tariffs, and controlled service and housing inflation will allow the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates unchanged this year, rather than the market currently pricing in a total of about 1.5 interest rate hikes by the first quarter of next year.

Recent employment data have been weaker than expected, and Chairman Warsh said that "inflation risks have declined", which also strengthened the judgment that market interest rate expectations may be overly hawkish. Within the AI chain, Morgan Stanley believes that this round is not the end of the AI capital expenditure cycle, but another phased rotation.

The breadth of revisions to semiconductor earnings expectations is at a historical high, and the sector weakened after Micron's earnings report, indicating that the market is beginning to focus on the "peak rate of change" of earnings improvement; memory chips may continue to lead the decline due to their stronger commodity attributes.

At the same time, Meta announced the sale of excess computing power to external customers, which may prompt the market to re-evaluate the marginal growth rate of cloud vendors' capital expenditures.

In the short term, the bank prefers ultra-large-scale cloud vendors over semiconductors, believing that the former have a stable main business, AI application layer options and room for cost reduction.

In terms of industry allocation, the first choices are consumer goods, transportation and biotechnology; among them, biotechnology is sensitive to downward interest rates, with historical annualized returns approaching 20% in an environment of falling high interest rates, and can benefit from rising M&A activities.

ANZ: Lithium prices will still fall after rebounding, and supply surplus may reach 100,000 tons LCE in 2026 The latest report from Australia and New Zealand Banking Group (ANZ) points out that lithium prices have begun to fall since the sharp rebound in mid-2025.

With the gradual recovery of previously reduced production capacity and the slowdown in demand growth, the market oversupply will continue, and lithium prices are expected to fall back to around US$16,000/ton.

According to the report, this round of rise was first driven by China's tightening policies, followed by Australia's production cuts and Zimbabwe's restrictions on lithium concentrate exports in February, which further pushed up prices; energy storage demand supported consumption.

However, the current price is significantly higher than the production cost, and the supply side is more motivated to resume production. China's lepidolite production profits have improved.

If CATL's Ganxiawo Mine resumes production in 2027, it may increase the supply of lithium by approximately 46,000 tons; Australia's previously suspended projects such as Bald Hill, Ngungaju and Finniss are also restarting.

It is expected that local spodumene production will increase by 3% to 579,000 tons in 2026, and will increase by another 15% to 655,000 tons in 2027. In Zimbabwe, the export ban on lithium concentrate has been converted into a quota system.

Chinese-funded projects such as Bikita, Sabi Star and Arcadia have obtained quotas and resumed exports, further easing supply disturbances. On the demand side, electric vehicles still account for about 55% of global lithium consumption, but the proportion of energy storage systems has increased from 5% before the epidemic to about 20% this year.

However, U.S. policy uncertainty has dragged down electric vehicle sales. BloombergNEF data shows that global electric vehicle sales have reached 6.9 million units so far this year, down 6% year-on-year. ANZ expects global lithium demand growth to slow to 11% from 25% last year.

The bank predicts that the lithium market surplus will rise to 100,000 tons of LCE in 2026, equivalent to 6% of annual demand. Loose supply and demand will suppress prices and continue to decline.

JPMorgan Chase: Semiconductor industry revenue may exceed US$1.5 trillion in 2026, with rising prices for AI and storage driving high growth JPMorgan Chase's latest research report states that driven by the continued expansion of AI capital expenditures, sharp increases in storage product prices and the recovery of traditional cycles, the global

semiconductor industry's revenue is expected to exceed US$1.5 trillion in 2026, reaching US$1.5 trillion to US$1.6 trillion, a year-on-year increase of more than 90%.

The bank maintains an overweight rating on the semiconductor sector, believing that accelerated computing, storage and network equipment suppliers will most directly benefit from new AI investments.

WSTS data shows that global semiconductor sales in May reached US$131.9 billion, a month-on-month increase of 16.1%, significantly higher than the seasonal level of 4.5%; a year-on-year increase of 118.8%, further accelerating from 106.4% in April.

Industry sales excluding storage were US$57.5 billion, a slight increase of 0.6% month-on-month and a year-on-year increase of 34%, indicating that the recovery in demand excluding storage is still continuing.

In the past three months, industry revenue has increased by 104% year-on-year, and increased by 30.5% after excluding storage, both significantly improved from April. Price is the core driver of this round of growth.

In May, the average selling price of semiconductors increased by 16.7% month-on-month and 85% year-on-year, which was higher than the approximately 70% year-on-year increase in March. During the same period, overall shipments decreased slightly by 0.1% month-on-month, but still increased by 20.6% year-on-year.

Among them, DRAM prices increased by 14% month-on-month, and flash memory prices increased by 26%, driving sales of the two types of products to increase by 27.7% and 39.8% month-on-month respectively; analog chip and MCU prices fell by 3.5% and 4.5% respectively.

JPMorgan Chase said that before entering the second quarter financial reporting season, feedback from the industry chain showed that orders, backlogs and customer orders were still increasing, and prices for many types of products continued to rise.

The bank expects that improvements in customer and channel inventory conditions will drive the recovery of most end-market markets, but it is also wary of high storage prices and supply shortages, which may suppress demand in customer-based markets such as consumer electronics and PCs.

Goldman Sachs: New car sales in Europe's five major markets increased by 12% in June, and the increase in electric vehicle sales pushed BMW to buck the trend and outperform By power type, the sales of pure electric vehicles in the five major markets in June increased by 63% year-on-year, plug-in hybrid vehicles increased by 25%, and the implied sales of traditional fuel vehicles fell by 1% year-on-year.

The sales of pure electric vehicles in rolling three-month, six-month and twelve-month periods increased by 55%, 45% and 39% year-on-year respectively, driving the penetration rate of pure electric vehicles to increase from 19% in the first quarter to 24% in June and 22% in rolling three-month period, which is higher than the 17% level for the whole year of 2025.

In terms of car companies, the sales of Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz, Stellantis and Renault in June increased by 9.1%, decreased by 1.9%, increased by 4.6% and increased by 3.9% respectively; the rolling three-month growth rates in the second quarter were 3.6%, 1.7%, 2.3% and 0.7% respectively, which were all lower than the overall market.

Correspondingly, the market share of the four car companies in the second quarter fell by 103, 32, 91 and 76 basis points year-on-year respectively.

The BMW Group's rolling three-month sales in June and the second quarter increased by 14.4% and 9.2% respectively, mainly driven by Mini sales growth of 47% and 41% respectively, and market share increased by 15 and 8 basis points respectively.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that the increase in the penetration rate of electric vehicles is an important driving force for the growth of orders from European car companies in the first half of the year, but the pace of acceleration in the second half of the year still needs to be observed.

Renault said its electric vehicle orders remained strong in April and May and stabilized in June; order books in markets such as France and Germany once grew by about 50%, and the company is evaluating increasing electric vehicle production shifts in the second half of the year.

On the macro front, the Eurozone consumer confidence index rose to -17.7 in June, an improvement from -19.0 in May, but still weak.

Volkswagen, Stellantis and Renault also jointly proposed that the EU should promote the "Made in Europe" framework and require that 70% of the value of vehicles sold in the EU come from the region to strengthen the local industrial chain.

Goldman Sachs: The investment industry's operating environment was stable in the second quarter, and the rebound in mergers and acquisitions and financing supported performance growth Goldman Sachs’ latest research report stated that the operating environment of independent U.S. investment banks in the second quarter was generally healthy.

Although the market still has divergences on the sustainability of macroeconomic, geopolitical and M&A recovery, industry valuations have become somewhat attractive.

The bank predicts that the total revenue of covered investment banks will increase by 13%, 13% and 8% respectively from 2026 to 2028, and the pre-tax profit margin will increase by 130, 210 and 120 basis points respectively; the current price-earnings ratio for the next 12 months is about 16.5 times, which is about 60% in the past 11 years.

Goldman Sachs believes that the M&A cycle is still in the middle. The scale of industry mergers and acquisitions in the current cycle has increased by about 90% from a low point, completing approximately 55% of the average growth rate in the past four cycles. There may still be room for growth of 65 to 75 percentage points in the future.

However, the market showed a clear "two-speed differentiation": in the second quarter, the amount of strategic mergers and acquisitions and large-scale mergers and acquisitions increased by 89% and 202% year-on-year respectively, while private equity-backed mergers and acquisitions and medium-sized mergers and acquisitions only increased by 10% and 34%.

Since the beginning of the year, private equity M&A has only accounted for 23% of total M&A, which is lower than the average of 34% and 32% in the past 5 years and 10 years. However, in the long term, it is still driven by unexited existing assets, funds to be deployed and potential interest rate cuts. Financing business also forms an important support.

On an annualized basis, global ECM and DCM scale increased by 55% and 19% year-on-year respectively; AI capital expenditure is expected to reach US$770 billion in 2026, a year-on-year increase of approximately 85%. Goldman Sachs predicts that U.S.

IPO fundraising will rise to a record high of US$225 billion, 95% higher than the peak in 2021, and the corresponding IPO underwriting fee pool may reach US$4.5 billion to US$9 billion, a year-on-year increase of 89% to 279%. Among them, medical and health ECM has grown by 140% year-to-date, significantly higher than the industry’s growth rate of 68%.

In addition, secondary share trading and restructuring businesses provide stronger structural and countercyclical income.

Goldman Sachs estimates that the two businesses will together account for about 18% of the investment bank's revenue in 2025; secondary market fund raising increased by 58% year-on-year in the first quarter, while the proportion of unrealized value of private equity funds for more than five years has risen to 54%.

Goldman Sachs prefers Evercore, Houlihan Lokey, and Lazard, believing that they will benefit from strategic mergers and acquisitions, restructuring, and long-term share valuation business growth respectively.

Goldman Sachs: Medicare liberalizes GLP-1 reimbursement for weight loss, which may bring in $8 billion in revenue in 2027 According to the latest research report from Goldman Sachs, the US Medicare CMS launched the "GLP-1 Bridge" temporary project on July 1.

By December 2027, eligible Medicare (Federal Medical Insurance) beneficiaries can obtain Novo Nordisk's Wegovy injection and oral medicine, Eli Lilly's Zepbound Kwikpen and Foundayo oral medicine for weight loss treatment by paying a monthly deductible of US$50.

This policy breaks through Medicare's long-term restriction on not reimbursing pure weight loss drugs for the first time, and is expected to significantly expand the anti-obesity drug market.

A Goldman Sachs survey of 25 doctors found that the interviewed doctors expected that after the project goes online, about 31% of eligible patients will use GLP-1 drugs in the second half of 2026, and the penetration rate will rise to 45% about a year later.

It is estimated that there are approximately 15 million potentially qualified patients, which corresponds to approximately 3.5 million people receiving treatment in the initial stage of launch and approximately 7 million people receiving treatment after one year; in addition, 38% of qualified patients have expressed interest in GLP-1 weight loss treatment.

Goldman Sachs reminded that the doctors surveyed may be more active in prescribing drugs than the market average, and the actual penetration rate still needs to be observed.

In terms of product share, doctors predict that Eli Lilly's Zepbound Kwikpen will account for 44%, Novo Nordisk's Wegovy injection will account for 27%, Wegovy oral dosage will account for 16%, and Eli Lilly Foundayo will account for 13%.

Injections will account for about 70% in total, reflecting that doctors pay more attention to weight loss efficacy and tolerability, and the impact of oral or injection delivery methods is relatively limited. Notably, doctors did not see the fasting requirement for Wegovy oral medication as a major obstacle.

Goldman Sachs predicts that the Medicare channel will contribute approximately US$2.6 billion in revenue to anti-obesity drugs in 2026 and approximately US$8 billion in 2027. Taking into account price and volume factors, the potential incremental revenue in the second half of 2026 and 2027 will exceed US$2 billion and US$6 billion respectively.

The bank believes that the survey results are positive for Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, but pre-authorization is regarded as the biggest execution bottleneck, which may slow down early volume expansion.

HSBC: Tesla’s “unexpected jump” in deliveries in the second quarter is difficult to sustain and maintains its underweight rating HSBC's latest research report stated that Tesla delivered 480,000 vehicles in the second quarter, a year-on-year increase of 25% and a month-on-month increase of 34%, which was about 19% higher than HSBC and the market consensus expectations.

However, this "unexpected jump" is not consistent with the main market data it tracks, and there may be some demand in Europe, so the current delivery pace is unsustainable. HSBC maintained Tesla's "underweight" rating and slightly raised its target price from $123 to $125.

Based on the performance in the second quarter, HSBC raised Tesla's 2026 delivery forecast by about 93,000 vehicles, the full-year forecast by about 6%, and the 2027-2028 forecast by 2% to 3%. The corresponding 2026 revenue forecast was raised by 3% to 4%, and the operating profit and gross profit forecasts were raised by about 4%.

However, the bank emphasized that this adjustment mainly reflected a one-time exceedance of expectations in the second quarter and did not significantly increase subsequent operating assumptions. The energy storage business was lower than expected. Energy storage deployment in the second quarter was 13.5GWh.

Although it increased by 40.6% year-on-year and 53.4% month-on-month, it was still 18% lower than HSBC expected. The cumulative growth rate in the first half of this year was only 12%, significantly lower than the 40% previously expected.

As a result, HSBC has lowered its full-year energy storage deployment forecast, but still assumes a year-on-year growth rate of 40% to 50% in the second half of the year. Regarding FSD, Robotaxi and Optimus, which are highly concerned by the market, HSBC believes that Tesla's valuation still relies on the AI business that has not yet formed stable revenue.

It is cautious about the technological path of Robotaxi and the forward market capacity of humanoid robots and self-driving taxis. It believes that industrial change is more likely to be gradual rather than rapid realization.

Bernstein raises ASML target price to $2,623: AI production expansion drives demand for advanced lithography upwards Bernstein’s latest research report believes that the expansion of advanced logic chips and DRAM production capacity driven by artificial intelligence is significantly increasing ASML’s equipment demand.

In particular, high numerical aperture extreme ultraviolet lithography (High NA EUV) technology is expected to be the first to achieve large-scale application in memory chip manufacturing. The agency maintained ASML's "outperform" rating and raised its U.S. stock target price from $1,971 to $2,623.

Analyst David Dai said that the expansion of production brought by AI has exceeded previous expectations, and Bernstein has therefore significantly raised ASML's revenue forecast. The report points out that high-NA EUV may be adopted first by DRAM manufacturers rather than advanced logic chip manufacturers.

The core reason is that the exposure cost of DRAM manufacturing is relatively lower and the introduction of new technologies is more economical. ASML is a major supplier of high-NA EUV lithography equipment.

This equipment can etch finer circuit patterns on silicon wafers and is an important tool for manufacturing high-performance memory chips such as advanced DRAM and HBM.

As the demand for large model training and inference continues to grow, the importance of HBM and other high-end memories has increased, and the expansion of advanced production capacity by storage manufacturers is expected to become a new growth support for ASML.

Over the past year, ASML's stock price has risen by 123%, driven by the expansion of AI applications and improved expectations for semiconductor capital expenditures.

Bernstein believes that the simultaneous expansion of production in the advanced logic and storage fields will continue to gain upward momentum for the company's orders, revenue and profit expectations. Wall Street overall also maintained an optimistic view. LSEG data shows that all 19 analysts currently covering ASML have a "buy" or "strong buy" rating.

The market's subsequent attention will focus on the actual introduction pace of high-NA EUV in the DRAM field, and whether AI-related.

🌐 Nifty's 516-Point Capitulation: A Deep Technical Post-Mortem
The Indian equity markets faced a sharp correction on Wednesday, July 8, 2026. The benchmark Nifty 50 plummeted 516.65 points (-2.12%) to settle at 23,882.05, entirely erasing its recent multi-day winning streak. Bank Nifty mirrored this weakness, shedding 2.51% to close at 56,742.60. For experienced traders, this session marked a clear short-term Change of Character (ChoCH), shifting immediate control to the bears.
📊 Technical Structure & Indicators
The index sliced through both its 20 EMA (~23,992) and 50 EMA (~23,921) on the daily chart, turning former dynamic support bands into immediate overhead supply walls. Today's price action formed a long Bearish Marubozu candle on high volume, closing near the absolute session low of 23,805.20. The Daily RSI (14) plummeted to 36.5, signaling heavy downward momentum without any immediate bullish divergence.
📰 Geopolitical Shockwaves
This sell-off was a news-driven event triggered by severe overnight geopolitical flare-ups in West Asia. Fresh military strikes caused Brent crude to jump over 3.5%, crossing $78 per barrel. This macro shift reignited local inflation fears, dragging down high-beta heavyweights like Reliance (-2.48%) and HDFC Bank (-2.29%). Consequently, the India VIX surged 25% to 14.54, reflecting immediate market panic.
🛠️ Key Levels & Playbook
Immediate Resistance: 24,000
Major Resistance: 24,150
Immediate Support: 23,800
Major Demand Floor: 23,500
If Nifty breaks below 23,800 tomorrow, expect a fast slide toward 23,500. Conversely, a sustained recovery back above 24,000 is required to trigger a relief short-squeeze. Cut trading sizes by 50% to manage the heightened volatility.
This article for Educational purposes only.

🔴 $QQQ $707.61 (-0.26%)
Trump stated he would hate to strike desalination plants in Iran but said he may have to, marking a direct threat against Iranian civilian infrastructure. WTI crude is surging 10.73% to $75.29 on the geopolitical escalation, with the VIX jumping 9.80% to $17.71 as markets price in risk across equities and the SPY sliding 0.58%.

The headline says BOTH_BUYING_RISK_ON. The tape says something very different. Nifty down over 2%, Bank Nifty down 2.5%, zero — literally zero — Bank Nifty constituents advancing, and VIX exploding 26% in a single session. This is not a risk-on day. This is a day where the regime label is being stress-tested in real time.

FII cash net of ₹1,963 Cr looks constructive on the surface — fourth day of buying, MTD now at ₹2,502 Cr. But zoom out: the 30-day average daily FII flow is -₹2,407 Cr. Today's print is 0.3x that average in absolute terms. This is not conviction buying. And when you layer in the derivatives picture, the story flips entirely. FII sold ₹4,807 Cr of index futures against that ₹1,963 Cr cash buy — combined net exposure change is -₹2,844 Cr. FIIs are buying cash and aggressively shorting futures. That's not accumulation, that's a hedged position being built while the market falls.

The FII index futures net short now sits at 2.69 lakh contracts with ₹52,609 Cr OI — that short book has grown, not shrunk. Vol net of -29,748 contracts confirms this is positional, not intraday noise. The 'buying streak' narrative is being actively undermined by what FIIs are doing in derivatives.

Brent crude surging over 6.5% overnight is the macro trigger here. For India as an oil importer, a move of that magnitude in a single session is a direct hit to the current account, a potential inflation re-rating, and an equity risk premium shock. The overnight S&P close was negative, Nasdaq down over 1% — the global tape was already soft before crude piled on.

Breadth is catastrophic. 8% of Nifty 50 stocks advancing, 0% of Bank Nifty, 6% of Smallcap 100. Smallcap sits at 87% of its 52-week range — near highs — with virtually nobody buying. Nifty IT at just 13% of its 52-week range, down 3.83% over 30 days, and still no buyers even with the index selling off. That's a sector in structural distress, not a dip-buying opportunity yet.

DII net of ₹790 Cr is the weakest DII print in weeks — well below the 30-day average of ₹3,933 Cr. The domestic cushion is thin today. The market is falling through DII support, not being held by it. The VIX at 14.68 with a 26% single-day spike is the clearest signal — this is fear entering the market, not complacency. The question now is whether FIIs use this dip to cover shorts or press them.

Just in: Trump said the US-Iran ceasefire was over, US stock futures fell collectively, and international oil prices soared: What to watch tonight.

On Wednesday, as the situation in the Middle East escalated again, international oil prices soared and U.S. stock index futures fell sharply. Major European indexes generally fell. (Source: Yingwei Finance) U.S.

President Donald Trump said at a NATO summit in Türkiye on Wednesday that the ceasefire with Iran was "over." Trump's remarks came after the United States struck more than 80 targets in Iran on Tuesday and took steps to cut off Iran's legal oil exports in response to Iran's previous attacks on ships near the Strait of Hormuz.

Brent crude oil futures, the global oil price benchmark, once rose by more than 5%, standing at $78 per barrel. Energy stocks were broadly higher in premarket trading. ConocoPhillips and Marathon Petroleum rose nearly 2%, while Chevron and Exxon Mobil rose more than 1%. Chip stocks extended recent losses.

Micron Technology fell nearly 4% before the market opened. As of Tuesday's close, its stock price had fallen 25% from its 52-week high. Daniela Hathorn, senior market analyst at https://t.co/H9WqjFayXa, said: "Renewed tensions in the Middle East have interrupted the market's increasingly complacent narrative.

In the past few weeks, investors have been betting that the situation will calm down smoothly, and now they have to reassess geopolitical risks." Investors will also pay attention to the minutes of the Federal Reserve's June Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting released at 2 pm ET on Wednesday (2 am Beijing time on Thursday).

The market expects that the meeting minutes will further reveal the details of discussions when Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh presides over his first policy meeting. At the time, the Fed kept interest rates unchanged while signaling that further interest rate hikes may still be necessary in the future if inflationary pressures persist.

Adam Crisafulli, founder of Vital Knowledge, said in the report: "The FOMC meeting minutes may become an 'unknown variable' because Warsh revealed little in his latest press conference." He said: "Normally, (former chairman) Jerome Powell would give a fairly comprehensive explanation of what was discussed at the meeting, but Warsh did not do that, so the

minutes of the meeting, which is likely to be hawkish, may bring some unexpected information." [Apple and Broadcom sign an agreement exceeding US$30 billion to increase US chip manufacturing] On July 8, Apple announced a multi-year cooperation agreement with Broadcom, with a total amount expected to exceed US$30 billion.

The two parties will jointly design and produce customized chips and advanced wireless connection technologies for multiple Apple products.

Under the agreement, Broadcom will produce more than 15 billion chips in the United States and invest US$1.5 billion to expand and upgrade production capacity at its Fort Collins, Colorado, factory to produce advanced radio frequency components and wireless connection technologies including FBAR radio frequency filters.

Apple said this is the largest cooperation project to date under its "Made in America Plan" and is part of its commitment to invest $600 billion in the U.S. economy over the next four years, aiming to promote the construction of the domestic semiconductor supply chain in the United States and support employment.

[OpenAI will officially release the GPT-5.6 series on the 9th] OpenAI announced that it will officially publicly release the GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna versions on July 9, local time.

[Musk: SpaceXAI will open Grok 4.5 to the public tomorrow] Musk posted on the social media platform X on July 8 that based on the positive feedback from internal beta users, SpaceXAI will open Grok 4.5 to the public tomorrow. Musk said that this is an Opus-level model, but it is faster, more token efficient and lower cost.

[SpaceX applies to deploy a third-generation satellite constellation composed of 100,000 satellites] According to the U.S. Federal Communications Commission (FCC), SpaceX has formally applied for FCC approval this week to launch and operate a third-generation (Gen3) satellite constellation consisting of 100,000 satellites.

According to reports, the third-generation constellation will consist of 100,000 satellites, deployed in two sets of thin, closely stacked orbital shells, with nominal altitudes ranging from 323 to 327.5 kilometers and 473 to 477.5 kilometers respectively.

SpaceX describes the third-generation constellation as "a powerful, resilient, ubiquitous communications infrastructure" with "the ability to carry most of the world's Internet traffic", providing extremely low-latency, multi-gigabit-level uplink and downlink symmetric throughput capabilities for global government users, enterprises, consumers, and billions of AI-driven devices.

[TSMC is reported to have significantly expanded PIC production capacity and is expected to reach 25,000 pieces per month in 2028] According to agency forecasts, TSMC’s photonic integrated circuit (PIC) production capacity will rise rapidly.

TSMC's production capacity will increase from the current level of about 500 pieces per month to 10,000 pieces per month in the second quarter of 2026, and will further increase to 15,000 pieces per month in the fourth quarter, and is expected to increase to at least 25,000 pieces per month in 2028.

A Preview of Surging Oil Prices and an Inflation Shock: A Multi-AI Decision Framework

After Trump’s remarks, oil prices surged more than 6% in a single day, as markets began pricing in the possibility of disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and a sharp increase in the risk of crude supply interruptions. This was not merely a short-term oil-price reaction, but a live stress test for inflation expectations, central bank credibility, and global asset allocation.

To assess the situation more objectively, this analysis uses a multi-AI decision framework rather than relying on a single directional view. Different AI models are used to evaluate the same event from multiple angles, including geopolitical escalation risk, oil supply disruption probability, inflation transmission, central bank reaction functions, cross-asset pricing, and market sentiment. The purpose is not to predict one fixed outcome, but to build a scenario-based decision map and identify where market pricing may be too optimistic or too complacent.

Global institutional investors quickly adjusted their positioning, increasing exposure to energy stocks while reducing highly valued growth stocks as a defensive move. Retail sentiment indicators showed panic spreading, further intensifying selling pressure, while social media was filled with concerns about inflation spiraling out of control.

Federal Reserve officials delivered hawkish comments, emphasizing that an oil-price shock would push inflation higher. As a result, market expectations for rate cuts in 2026 fell sharply. The Bank of Japan also came under pressure and may be forced to raise rates earlier than expected to contain yen depreciation, further widening the divergence in global monetary policy.

From a multi-AI perspective, the key conclusion is clear: an energy supply shock has already transmitted rapidly into financial markets. If central banks are forced to extend the hawkish cycle, risk assets may face structural valuation pressure.

The Fragility of Ceasefires and Market Game Theory

Although ceasefire agreements in the Middle East have been reached several times, each breakdown has triggered violent swings in oil prices. After the latest event, international mediation efforts continued, but the hostile stance between Iran and the United States has made the ceasefire outlook highly uncertain.

In this framework, multiple AI models are used to challenge each other’s assumptions. One model focuses on geopolitical probability and concludes that the market may be underestimating the risk of escalation. Another model focuses on historical ceasefire patterns and suggests that repeated breakdowns often lead to larger risk premiums in oil. A third model looks at market positioning and argues that if investors are already heavily hedged, a ceasefire could trigger a sharp reversal rally in risk assets.

This creates a divided market. One side expects another ceasefire agreement to be reached, betting on a pullback in oil prices and a rebound in risk assets. The other side believes an escalation of conflict is inevitable and has moved early into safe-haven assets such as gold and the US dollar.

Energy traders are closely monitoring shipping data in the Strait of Hormuz. Any sign of a blockade could trigger another surge in oil prices, potentially pushing crude above $100 per barrel. Central banks are struggling to balance hawkish policy stances against slowing economic growth, and mixed policy signals are increasing market volatility, with the VIX spiking.

The multi-AI decision framework therefore treats this as a game of probability, not certainty. The real edge is not in guessing whether oil must rise or fall, but in identifying which scenario the market has not fully priced in.

Tail Risks and Structural Shifts

In an extreme scenario, if the ceasefire collapses completely, oil prices could break above $100 and trigger a global inflation crisis. The Federal Reserve could be forced to raise rates, leading to a stock market crash and credit-market contraction, similar to a repeat of the 2022 playbook.

However, contrarian AI models highlight an important counterpoint: excessive pessimism may overlook the tail opportunity of a ceasefire agreement. Once risks ease, a rapid pullback in oil prices could trigger an oversold rebound, especially in technology stocks and long-duration bonds.

From a structural perspective, energy-security concerns will accelerate green-transition investment across countries. However, in the short term, demand for fossil fuels may surge, and the energy sector could continue to benefit. Global institutional investors will reassess asset allocation and increase hedging positions to manage tail risks, while retail investors may worsen market volatility through panic behavior.

This preview shows why a single-model or single-narrative approach is dangerous. A multi-AI framework forces investors to compare trend-following logic with contrarian logic, macro logic with positioning logic, and geopolitical risk with market-implied probabilities. In a market driven by oil, inflation, and central banks, one-directional conviction can become the biggest risk.

Multi-AI Scenario Tree

Bearish Scenario — 50%

Trump’s hardline comments on Iran lead to a complete breakdown of the ceasefire. Middle East geopolitical tensions escalate, shipping through the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted, and the risk of crude supply interruption surges.

Rising oil prices push global inflation expectations higher → the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Japan maintain or strengthen hawkish policy → the US dollar index strengthens → US equities and long-duration Treasuries come under valuation pressure. The energy sector benefits, while gold receives safe-haven support but is constrained by a stronger dollar.

The geopolitical AI model gives this scenario the highest probability because the risk of miscalculation is rising. The macro AI model also supports this view, as higher oil prices would delay disinflation and strengthen the case for hawkish central banks.

Base Case — 30%

Geopolitical tensions remain elevated but manageable. Ceasefire negotiations make some progress but remain unstable. Oil prices rise due to supply concerns but fail to break key resistance levels.

Oil prices rise moderately and remain in the $80–90 range → inflation expectations rise slightly but do not spiral out of control → the Federal Reserve maintains a hawkish stance but does not raise rates → market volatility increases, and asset prices trade in a range.

The market-pricing AI model sees this as the most “comfortable” scenario for investors, because it allows markets to price risk without a full supply shock. However, the risk is that investors may become too complacent if the situation remains unresolved.

Bullish Scenario — 20%

A ceasefire agreement is reached again through international mediation. Geopolitical risk eases rapidly, and oil prices pull back as supply concerns fade.

Oil prices decline, bringing inflation expectations lower → expectations for hawkish Federal Reserve policy ease → the US dollar weakens → US equities and long-duration Treasuries recover in valuation. The energy sector comes under pressure.

The contrarian AI model assigns value to this scenario because market fear can become overextended. If the ceasefire headline arrives when investors are heavily positioned for escalation, the rebound in SPY, QQQ, and TLT could be sharp.

AI Projection vs Market Pricing — Polymarket

Will WTI crude oil break above $90 before year-end?
Market: 34% · Multi-AI Projection: 60%
The multi-AI framework assigns a higher probability because the geopolitical escalation scenario remains significant, while the market may be underpricing the tail risk of a ceasefire breakdown. The key divergence is that market pricing may still be too optimistic about diplomacy, while the AI scenario tree gives more weight to supply-disruption risk.

Will there be no Fed rate cuts in 2026?
Market: 78% · Multi-AI Projection: 85%

The multi-AI projection is broadly aligned with the market. A surge in oil prices would worsen inflation and force the Federal Reserve to maintain a hawkish policy stance, making the probability of no rate cuts high. However, if a ceasefire is reached and oil prices fall quickly, this probability could be revised lower.
Multi-AI Decision Framework

The investment decision is not based on one AI answer. It is based on combining several AI-generated views:

Geopolitical AI assesses the probability of ceasefire breakdown, military escalation, and disruption in the Strait of Hormuz.

Macro AI evaluates the impact of oil prices on inflation expectations, central bank policy, bond yields, and the US dollar.

Cross-Asset AI compares the likely reaction of SPY, QQQ, TLT, XLE, GLD, DXY, and energy equities.

Sentiment AI tracks panic, retail positioning, social-media fear, and volatility indicators such as the VIX.

Contrarian AI tests whether the market has become too bearish and whether a ceasefire headline could trigger a sharp reversal.

The final decision is made by comparing these models against market pricing. When AI probability is materially higher than market probability, the opportunity is in positioning for underpriced risk. When market pricing is already extreme, the priority shifts to risk management and fast reversal planning.

Key Indicators to Watch

Signs of shipping blockades or military escalation in the Strait of Hormuz

Hawkish Federal Reserve comments or stronger-than-expected core inflation data

Progress in Iran-US diplomatic talks and ceasefire draft agreements

Bank of Japan rate-decision statements and yen volatility

WTI crude oil breaking above the key resistance level of $90 per barrel

Global crude inventory reports and OPEC+ production decisions

VIX, MOVE Index, credit spreads, and retail sentiment indicators

Risk Warning

A full escalation of geopolitical conflict could disrupt crude supply, trigger a global recession, and cause a financial market crash.

Stronger-than-expected inflation could force central banks into a sudden policy shift, worsening market liquidity stress and credit tightening.

A renewed ceasefire could trigger a sharp pullback in oil prices and a major correction in energy stocks, exposing investors with insufficient hedging to losses.

The multi-AI framework does not eliminate uncertainty. Its value is that it helps investors avoid emotional decision-making, compare different scenarios, and adjust positions when market pricing changes. In this environment, the goal is not to be permanently bullish or bearish. The goal is to stay flexible, manage tail risk, and let probability guide the decision.

​The Problem: The Current Petroleum Chain
​Right now, standard nonwoven fabric is tightly bound to the oil market through this chain:
Because polypropylene is a direct byproduct of refining petroleum, whenever global political tensions or OPEC decisions cause crude oil prices to spike, Fiberweb’s raw material costs shoot up instantly. ​The Solution: Breaking the Chain via Agriculture
​In their new ₹160 Crore Spunlace project, Fiberweb is replacing petroleum-based plastic granules with plant-based, natural inputs:
​Wood Pulp & Cellulose (Viscose/Rayon): Derived organically from timber and plant stalks.
​Natural Cotton: Sourced directly from agricultural yields.
​Bamboo Fibers: A highly sustainable, fast-growing crop.
​Why this insulates the company from crude oil shocks:
​Decoupled Pricing: The price of a bale of cotton or wood pulp is governed by crop cycles, rainfall, and farming supply—not by geopolitical conflicts in oil-producing nations. If crude oil jumps to $100 a barrel tomorrow, plant fiber costs remain unaffected.
​Stable Margin Forecasting: Agricultural raw materials have far more predictable pricing cycles compared to the volatile daily swings of the crude oil market. This allows Fiberweb to sign long-term supply contracts with global buyers with locked-in profit margins.
​The "Green Premium": Western markets (US & Europe) are actively enforcing strict anti-plastic legislations. Major brands are willing to pay a 15% to 25% premium price for certified plastic-free, plant-based biodegradable wipes and medical drapes.
​In short: Fiberweb is moving its supply chain from underground oil reserves to above-ground agricultural fields. This simultaneously makes their product eco-friendly (biodegradable) and protects their balance sheet from energy-market volatility.
